events.title: Restoring Dignity: An Adult B'Mitzvah Service Project
events.description:
Did you know that for many women and girls in our community, basic necessities like bras, underwear, and menstrual products are often out of reach? “Period poverty” is a hidden crisis—these essential items are not covered by SNAP benefits and are rarely donated to local food pantries. Without them, women and girls are often forced to miss work or school, compromising their health and their dignity. 
As our Adult B’Mitzvah class prepares for this meaningful milestone, we are honored to partner with Dignity Matters, a local non-profit dedicated to providing these essential items to women and girls experiencing homelessness or extreme poverty. This drive is a beautiful expression of the values at the heart of our Jewish journey—tzedakah, kavod habriot (honoring the dignity of every person), and tikkun olam. 
How You Can Help: Throughout the month of May, please consider donating new, unopened items to support our neighbors in need. There are three easy ways to participate: 
1. Drop Off at TBE—Bring donations to the Dignity Matters bin in the TBE Atrium anytime during the month of May. 
2. Shop the Amazon Wish List—Order items directly and have them shipped to the Dignity Matters warehouse. 
3. Spread the Word—Tell your friends and family about the drive—every donation makes a difference! 
Most Needed Items: 

Menstrual Products: Pads (all sizes/absorbency), tampons, and liners. Open packages are acceptable if items are individually wrapped. Most needed.
Underwear: Must be new and in original packaging. All sizes welcome—adult S, M, and L are most needed.
Bras: New or gently used (laundered and in excellent condition). Any style welcome—bralettes, sports bras, and regular bras.

Thank you for supporting our class project and helping us ensure that every woman and girl in our community can live with the dignity she deserves. 
Sponsored by TBE’s Adult B’Mitzvah Program

events.title: Collection |  Hunger Doesn't Take a Summer Vacation
events.description:
Did you know that the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE provides 80 TBE Table-prepared meals to students at the William Monroe Trotter School every month during the school year? These nutritious meals, alongside the school’s free breakfast and lunch program, make up a significant portion of many children’s daily diet. 
But as summer approaches and school year programs pause, that safety net disappears. No child should go hungry—and with your help, we can bridge the gap. 
Here are three ways you can contribute: 
1. Shop the Amazon Wish List Order items directly from our Amazon Wish List and have them delivered straight to TBE. Easy, convenient, and impactful! 
2. Contribute to the RJI Fund Make a financial contribution to the Racial Justice Initiative Fund to support this and other ongoing efforts. 
3. Purchase and Drop Off Items at the TBE Atrium Pick up any of the following preferred items and bring them to the TBE Atrium: 

Chef Boyardee Spaghetti & Meatballs Microwaveable Bowls
General Mills/Kellogg Cereal Single Serve Pouches
Nature Valley Granola Bars
Nutri-Grain Breakfast Bars
Quaker Instant Oatmeal Express Cups
Velveeta Shells & Cheese Microwaveable Cups
Welch’s Fruit Snacks

This event is sponsored by the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE, which works to increase our understanding and activism to address ongoing discrimination and inequality.

events.title: Boston Scottish Fiddle Club Gala
events.description:
Event date: June 7, 2026 
Event Time: 03:00 PM - 06:00 PM
Location: 
20 Junction Street
Needham, MA 02492
Description:
The Boston Scottish Fiddle Club is excited to announce their eighth annualGala scheduled for Sunday, June 7 from 3:00pm-6:00pm at the VFW post, 20 Junction St., Needham, MA! The event incudes a Scottish music concert, featuring the Boston Scottish Fiddle orchestra and featured guest Ed Pearlman.  Ceilidh Dancing, and a silent auction. will follow the concert. Suggested donation is $20 at the door, kids 12 and under are free.The concert features the Boston Scottish Fiddle Orchestra, which is the performing group of the Boston Scottish Fiddle Club. Under the direction of Neil Pearlman the orchestra performs a repertoire of traditional and contemporary Scottish and Cape Breton tunes arranged to create a unique fresh sound while also keeping with the tradition.  Special guest Ed Pearlman has performed, taught, and promoted the fiddle music of Scotland and Cape Breton for over 45 years throughout the US and in Canada and Scotland and directed the Boston Scottish Fiddle Club 1981-1999

events.title: Memorial Day Parade and Ceremony
events.description: Parade route and schedule 8:00-8:15am—Parade formation on South Ave., between Main Street and Washington St. 8:30am—Flag Ceremony at Moran Square at the Natick Center T-Station 8:45am—Parade marches to and around the Town Common Observances on the Common 9am Opening remarks at the Common John T. Martin, Veterans Services Officer Benediction- Chaplain, NSSC “National Anthem” by Natick High School Band Remarks from State Senator Karen Spilka’s office Remarks from State Representative David Linsky Remarks from Select Board Chair—Kathryn Coughlin Placing wreath at the Grand Army of Republic Monument by Scouts Keynote Speaker—Dave Josselyn, Vietnam Veteran “America the Beautiful” performed by Natick High Chorus Students “Taps” performed by Natick High School Band Parade forms up in front of the Common and marches: From East Central St. to West Central St. to Forest Ave. to Pond St. Parade marches and stops along the route for moment of silence and Taps, in honor of the fallen service members at: Old Dell Park Cemetery (163 Pond St.) St. Patrick’s Cemetery (180 Pond St.) Parade marches back to center of town and disbands All Veterans and Active Military are welcome to march in the parade. Please be advised that road closures will be in effect to accommodate parade traffic. In case of inclement weather, updates will be posted on the town website, and a decision regarding the parade will be made by 7am. If the parade needs to be moved indoors, the indoor location will be at the Natick Community Senior Center, located at 117 East Central St., Natick, at 9am. Thank you to Veterans Services Officer John Martin for this information.

events.title: Postponed: TBElle | Laughing Through the Ages: Jewish Humor, Resilience and Survival (Hybrid)
events.description:
This event has been postponed to June 2nd. You can find class by clicking here. Please reach out to the event Lynn Burke Harrell with any questions. 
This engaging course explores how Jewish communities have long turned to humor—rich with wit, irony, and a keen sense of the absurd—as a powerful way to endure hardship and persecution. Through key moments in Jewish history, we’ll discover how laughter has lived alongside suffering, fostering resilience, offering hope, and illuminating the depth and spirit of the Jewish experience. 
Dates: April 28, May 5, 12, 19, (May 26 postponed) June 2, June 9 
Join us for one, two, or however many classes you’d like to participate in! 
Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. 
This program is a part of TBElle (Temple Beth Elohim Lifelong Learning Experience). For over ten years, TBElle has been a vibrant community where congregants learn and grow together. This relaxed and fun experience is led by you—our fellow TBE members—who are eager to share their knowledge, experiences, stories, and talents. Discover new passions, connect with others, and contribute your unique perspective. Come participate and enrich our shared journey!

events.description:
This class has been postponed to June 9th. Please reach out to Lynn Burke Harrell with any questions. 
In this course, we’ll explore pivotal statements from the Catholic Church and Protestant churches that dramatically reshaped Christianity’s view of Judaism—alongside the Jewish responses to these changes. Together, we’ll consider the lasting impact of these developments and how contemporary issues continue to influence them, including their role in shaping today’s interfaith dialogue. 
Dates: Tuesdays, May 19, (May 26 is postponed to June 9), June 2, 9 
This course follows a sequential structure, but anyone is welcome to join at any point, or to drop in to any class. If you have questions regarding participation, please contact the program leader. 
Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register.

events.title: Text & Texture Ceramics Open Studios (In Person)
events.description:
We’ll use Jewish texts as inspiration for our work, enjoy snacks and drinks, and learn a wide variety of clay hand-building skills as well as decorative and finishing techniques. All pieces will be fired professionally in our TBE kiln. 
Feel free to utilize the full time or drop in for an hour or two. Whether you’re a beginner or have experience with clay, all skill levels are welcome. A trained ceramics guide will be present to support your journey! 
With your pass to our open studios you can drop in at any time during your chosen pass period. The pass isn’t limited to a set number of sessions; instead, it provides flexible access whenever you’re available. 
Since ceramics is an ongoing process that involves multiple stages—such as building, firing, and glazing—we’re unable to offer special pricing for individual sessions. 
Dates: Wednesdays, April 15, 29, May 6, 13, 20, 27, June 3, 10, 17, 24 
Registration: This event is available in person. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. Registration closes May 23 at 11:59 pm.

events.title: Drum Circle: Spring 2026 (In Person)
events.description:
Drum Circle start date has been postponed to April 16. 
Join us for an exciting, joyful drum circle with our own Friday night service drummer, Noam Sender. 
A drum circle is a centuries-old tradition tapping into age-old wisdom. Drumming is known to reduce stress and promote a sense of well being and mental alertness. Seated in a circle, we will play beautiful, accessible and captivating rhythms from around the world. The drumming will be enhanced with familiar chants and melodies (singing is optional). We will deepen the drumming experience with brief mindfulness exercises, learning to center ourselves, relax and explore our authentic self, while building a deep sense of community and sharing our collective voice. 
No prior experience required. Drums are available if needed, but feel free to bring your own if you like. Class size is limited to between 6 and 8 participants so it is best to register soon! 
This course follows a sequential structure, and participants are encouraged to attend all sessions. If you are interested in joining once the course has begun, please contact the program leader. 
Dates & Time: April 16, 23, May 7, 14, 28, June 4 
Fee: $180 
Registration: This program is held in person. Please click the purple button above to register. Registration closes Wednesday, April 16 at 11:59 pm. 
This program is part of The Well at TBE, Institute for Integrative Health, Wellness, and Spirituality. The Well at TBE integrates Jewish spiritual practices and modern mindfulness tools and techniques to provide a source of wellbeing through learning, practice, prayer, and support for individuals on their own and in community.

events.title: Adult Talmud Study | Daf Yomi (Online)
events.description:
Join us for a practice of reading and discussing a page of Talmud a day, led by Rabbi Carol Mitchell. A complete cycle takes seven years! We meet on-line on Friday mornings to study the most interesting and timely Talmud tractates. 
Daf Yomi is an international practice to read the entire Babylonian Talmud in seven and a half years at the rate of one page a day. Tens of thousands of Jews study Daf Yomi worldwide, all on the same page each day, following a schedule fixed in 1923 in Poland by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, the founder of Daf Yomi, who envisioned the whole world as a vast Talmudic classroom connected by a global network of conversational threads. Learn more 
Come when you can. Walk ins welcome. Prior registration is not required. 
If you are interested in learning with us or have questions, please contact Rabbi Carol Mitchell. All are welcome!

events.description:
Welcome Shabbat with uplifting music, meaningful prayer, and the warmth of community. As we gather in the sanctuary, we create sacred space for connection, reflection, and renewal—walking together on our shared path toward shalom. 
This evening, we will celebrate our Havayah seniors as they mark the milestone of Confirmation, sharing their voices and receiving the blessings of our community. We will also honor Rabbi Ben Rosen and the meaningful impact he has made through his leadership, care, and presence at TBE. 
Honor Rabbi Rosen by making a gift to the Youth Engagement Fund. 
Following services, Confirmation families are invited to continue the celebration with a potluck-style dinner. 
Click here to share lifecycle updates with the TBE Caring Connections team, letting us know if there is someone in need of a r’fuah shleimah, a full recovery of body and spirit, or other pastoral care. We would also love to celebrate joyous shehecheyanu moments with you, from the birth of a new baby to a retirement and everything in between.

events.description: For the third straight year, one of New England’s leading reenacting units, The King’s Own (4th Foot), is bringing its mix of history and fun to Needham Town Common May 30, 9 am-1 pm. See Redcoats drilling and preparing for war. Learn about equipment, organization, tactics, and weapons. Come face to face with soldiers sent to prevent or stop the American Revolution, and participate in an interactive debate on independence. Kids will have the chance to learn to march and handle (child safe!) muskets. Sponsored by the Needham Council for Arts and Culture.

events.title: King’s Own Come to Needham!
events.description: The British Are Coming! On Saturday, May 30th Needham will once again host The King’s Own, a group of reenactors who portray the British Redcoats who sought to prevent the American Revolution. Adults, teens, and children are invited to attend the fun, educational event that helps commemorate the 250th Anniversary of the founding of our country. See authentic uniforms, weapons, and drill, ask questions, and learn about the challenging days that led ordinary people of Needham into direct confrontation with their King and his army. Learn how British soldiers lived, fought, and made their way though seven years of warfare from Canada to the Caribbean. This event is sponsored by the Needham Cultural Council and is free to all.

events.title: Shabbat Morning Service (Hybrid)
events.description:
Our Shabbat service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Solin Meyers.
 
Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time.


About Solin
Hi everyone! My name is Solin, and I’m a 7th grader at Dover-Sherborn Middle School. My favorite subject in school is math (although recess is definitely the best!). Outside of school, I love spending time with my friends and family—especially playing with my chocolate lab puppy, Vinny. One of my favorite things to do is travel. I love exploring new places, trying new things, and going on exciting adventures. I also enjoy playing sports. Over the years, I’ve played basketball, volleyball, soccer, and softball. People always ask me which one is my favorite, but I honestly love them all. Being part of a team—supporting each other and bringing out the best in one another—makes me really happy. If you asked the people in my life to describe me, I think they would say I’m energetic, caring, and competitive. I feel so grateful for my family and friends, who make me excited to wake up each day and see what’s ahead.

For my ma’asim tovim project, I wanted to focus on something meaningful to me: helping people going through difficult times. After raising nearly $2,000, I started a project called Hope for Healing. With help from the TBE community, I created comfort baskets for kids in the emergency room at local hospitals like Newton-Wellesley. I hope to continue doing projects like this, because it makes me happy to know I can make a difference in someone’s life. 
Becoming a bat mitzvah means more to me than just leading a service. It means becoming part of a meaningful tradition and honoring those who came before me. TBE has helped me stay connected to my Jewish identity through its activities and the strong friendships I’ve built there. 
I’m so excited to celebrate this special day with all of you!

events.title: Shabbat Morning Service (Hybrid)
events.description:
Our Shabbat service includes the celebration of the B’Mitzvah of Emma Andler 
Registration: This event is available in person and via live stream. Registration is not required. Click here to join the live stream at the event start time. 
About Emma
Hi TBE. My name is Emma Andler and I am very excited to become a bat mitzvah. I am a 7th grader at Dana Hall School where my two favorite subjects are English and Social Studies. I live in Wellesley with my Mom, Elizabeth; my Dad, Rob; my little brother Jack who is ten; my little sister Lily who is six; and our two female guinea pigs, Figgy and Zippy. My grandparents, Susan and Julian Lange, live down the street from me, and we are very close. They are long-time members of TBE and my Mom grew up going to TBE and even used to teach Hebrew School here! 
Some things I enjoy doing are reading, hanging out with my friends, and playing with my younger siblings. I also love horseback riding, ice skating, and Broadway musicals. Singing and playing the piano are two of my hobbies. I’ve been in some musicals and even played Anna in Frozen which was all in Hebrew!! I have also helped with costume design in some other productions, including Mean Girls, Jr. and Legally Blonde, Jr. at Dana Hall. I have gone to Grossman Camp for many years and love it there too. 
I am so grateful to have met so many fantastic people at TBE. I even met some of my best friends here! 
Leading with kindness is very important to me. Kindness is key when leading if you want a community that accepts and includes people as they are. For my bat mitzvah project, I am serving as a Madricha (teacher’s aide) for a class of kindergartners and first graders at Kesher Newton and am teaching them Hebrew and Jewish values. Once I have become a bat mitzvah, I hope to continue teaching younger kids about Judaism and life as a Jewish person and I hope to continue to stay involved in the Jewish community as well.

About Avery
Dear TBE Community, my name is Avery Frieze. I love to play all kinds of sports, including fencing, pickleball, soccer, basketball, flag football, golf and tennis. Sports keep me active, strong and it’s fun to get to know new people. 
I am the youngest in my family by a minute. I have two sisters, one is my identical twin and one is my older sister. I have two parents, my mom and dad, and one absolutely adorable dog named Ottie. She is a golden retriever and I love her so much. 
I am in 7th grade at Carroll School, but next year I will join my sister at Beaver Country Day. I like math the best because it is one of my strengths. 
I have made one of my closest friends at school through my parents and TBE. I really like going to Rosh Hashana and Yom Kippur services. I love all of the singing and how everyone’s voices are so good and how everyone is synchronized. I also like the speeches that are low-key funny. 
Being a leader in my community makes me feel more mature and responsible. Becoming a bat mitzvah means that I am a Jewish adult and I am responsible for living with Jewish values and changing the world by helping people. 
About Marin
Dear TBE Community, my name is Marin Frieze. I am a soccer player and I play for my school, my town and my club. My family is the best. I am an identical twin, I have an older sister, two parents and one dog. Even though I am a twin, I am technically the middle child because I was born one minute ahead of my sister. 
I go to Beaver Country Day School and I am in the 7th grade. I like visual arts because it’s a class where we get to make sculptures and have fun. I love hanging out with my friends and playing soccer at recess, and I like math because I think I am good at problem solving. 
When I think about TBE, I think about my b’mitzvah tutor, Geoffrey. He is really funny and he has made Hebrew easy and fun to learn. He has been helpful and supportive by making sure I stay on track. 
I am so excited to be a Jewish adult. This means a lot to me because I get to become a leader in our community and set a good example by being a good student, a person who gives back and someone who stays connected to their Judaism. 
For my bat mitzvah, I collected clothing to donate to Cradles to Crayons and then went and volunteered to sort clothing and make care packages with outfits for teenage girls. It was fun to create all of the different outfits and package them knowing someone would open them and be happy. I also volunteered delivering food to the elderly with Family Table.

events.title: Craft with Wellesley Middle School Girl Scouts
events.description: Craft with Middle School Girl Scouts Sunday, May 31, 4:30 – 5:30 pm 5th – 7th graders snack & craft Adult orientation at 5:15 pm RSVP or ask questions: gstroop73505@gmail.com Fun & Friendship for Middle School Girls Wellesley Girl Scouts for 6th–8th graders combines fun, friendship, and adventure with community service. Scouts may also choose to pursue their Silver Award, a girl-led “take action” project which improves their community. Our troop meets monthly on Sundays late afternoon with one additional weekend activity scheduled each month. Participation is flexible based on your scout’s interests and schedule; our members attend various schools.This year’s activities have included: Community Service: archery and ice skating for younger girls, holiday party for Wellesley senior residents, and collecting food for Wellesley food Pantry. Scouts earned their animal helper badge by learning about the animals Unity Farm Sanctuary and crafting toys for Buddy Dog and Stray Pets in Need. Outings: apple picking, night climbing for Halloween, skiing at Wachusett, and back yard winter camping. The troop attended International Women’s Day at Mass Bay. We enjoyed the penguins at New England Aquarium and cannolis at Bova’s Bakery. The troop also bonded at beach volleyball, an arcade escape room, and cheered the Boston Legacy women’s soccer team to victory at Gillette Stadium. We will finish the year with a trip to Canobie Lake Park funded by our cookie sales. To RSVP or ask questions, please email gstroop73505@gmail.com Wellesley Girl Scouts are also recruiting girls at all grade levels (K-12) to form new troops or join existing troops in the fall. If you’re interested in joining Girl Scouts, please contact wellesleygs@gmail.com or customercare@gsema.org

events.title: Text & Texture Ceramics Open Studios (In Person)
events.description:
Our open ceramics studio is a space to connect and create in a relaxed and supportive atmosphere. Show up to make and connect alongside friends new and old 
We’ll use Jewish texts as inspiration for our work, enjoy snacks and drinks, and learn a wide variety of clay hand-building skills as well as decorative and finishing techniques. All pieces will be fired professionally in our TBE kiln. 
Feel free to utilize the full time or drop in for an hour or two. Whether you’re a beginner or have experience with clay, all skill levels are welcome. A trained ceramics guide will be present to support your journey! 
With your pass to our open studios you can drop in at any time during your chosen pass period. The pass isn’t limited to a set number of sessions; instead, it provides flexible access whenever you’re available. 
Since ceramics is an ongoing process that involves multiple stages—such as building, firing, and glazing—we’re unable to offer special pricing for individual sessions. 
Dates: April 6, April 13, April 27, May 4, May 11, May 18, June 1, June 8, June 15, June 22

events.title: TBElle | Laughing Through the Ages: Jewish Humor, Resilience and Survival (Hybrid)
events.description:
This engaging course explores how Jewish communities have long turned to humor—rich with wit, irony, and a keen sense of the absurd—as a powerful way to endure hardship and persecution. Through key moments in Jewish history, we’ll discover how laughter has lived alongside suffering, fostering resilience, offering hope, and illuminating the depth and spirit of the Jewish experience. 
Dates: April 28, May 5, 12, 19, (May 26 postponed), June 2, 9 
Join us for one, two, or however many classes you’d like to participate in! 
Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. 
This program is a part of TBElle (Temple Beth Elohim Lifelong Learning Experience). For over ten years, TBElle has been a vibrant community where congregants learn and grow together. This relaxed and fun experience is led by you—our fellow TBE members—who are eager to share their knowledge, experiences, stories, and talents. Discover new passions, connect with others, and contribute your unique perspective. Come participate and enrich our shared journey!

events.title: Dramatic Changes in Christianity’s View of Judaism After the Holocaust (Hybrid)
events.description:
In this course, we’ll explore pivotal statements from the Catholic Church and Protestant churches that dramatically reshaped Christianity’s view of Judaism—alongside the Jewish responses to these changes. Together, we’ll consider the lasting impact of these developments and how contemporary issues continue to influence them, including their role in shaping today’s interfaith dialogue.

events.title: Text & Texture Ceramics Open Studios (In Person)
events.description:
We’ll use Jewish texts as inspiration for our work, enjoy snacks and drinks, and learn a wide variety of clay hand-building skills as well as decorative and finishing techniques. All pieces will be fired professionally in our TBE kiln. 
Feel free to utilize the full time or drop in for an hour or two. Whether you’re a beginner or have experience with clay, all skill levels are welcome. A trained ceramics guide will be present to support your journey! 
With your pass to our open studios you can drop in at any time during your chosen pass period. The pass isn’t limited to a set number of sessions; instead, it provides flexible access whenever you’re available. 
Since ceramics is an ongoing process that involves multiple stages—such as building, firing, and glazing—we’re unable to offer special pricing for individual sessions. 
Dates: Wednesdays, April 15, 29, May 6, 13, 20, 27, June 3, 10, 17, 24

events.title: Slavery in Massachusetts: Then, and Its Legacy Today (Hybrid)
events.description:
Massachusetts is widely celebrated as the heart of the abolitionist movement—but there is a deeper, more complicated story that demands to be told. 
As we mark our nation’s 250th anniversary, TBE’s Racial Justice Initiative invites you to reckon with a history that is rarely acknowledged: the relationship between wealth and bondage in pre-Revolutionary Massachusetts. The Royall House and Slave Quarters in Medford was the plantation home of the Royalls—the largest slaveholding family in the Commonwealth, with at least 60 enslaved men, women, and children—existing in painful tension with Massachusetts’ Revolutionary fervor for freedom from tyranny. 
Joining us is Dr. Kyera Singleton, Executive Director of the Royall House and Slave Quarters, who will share the stories of those enslaved in the Greater Boston area before Massachusetts’ landmark 1783 court decision to abolish slavery. Dr. Singleton will also illuminate the remarkable resistance and activism of the enslaved and free Black people of that era—stories of courage and dignity that deserve to be heard and honored. 
This is history that lives in our own backyard, and as a community rooted in the values of justice, tzedakah, and repair, we are called to engage with it fully and honestly. 
This event is the companion experience to our June 7th tour of Royall House and Slave Quarters. Whether or not you attend that event, all are welcome to join us as we learn from Dr. Kyera Signleton. 
Registration: This event is available in person and online. Please click the appropriate purple bar above to register. 
This event is sponsored by the Racial Justice Initiative at TBE, which works to increase our understanding and activism to address ongoing discrimination and inequality.

events.title: Wellesley Trails Committee Guided Walk—Centennial Reservation
events.description: Join the Wellesley Trails Committee on a guided trail walk. Free. No registration required. Walks take place unless it pours rain or there are high winds. Dogs on leashes are welcome. DATE: Saturday, June 13 TIME: 10am-11:15am LOCATION: Meet at the Mass Bay parking lot off of Oakland Street, (not the Centennial lot) DESCRIPTION: Centennial Reservation (1.8 miles). Take a picturesque walk, through rolling meadows to the top of Maugus Hill, one of the highest points in Wellesley. View the beautiful Great Blue Hills in Canton from a hillside meadow overlooking the town of Needham. Then, descend through the meadow and around Benzanson Pond, a favorite swimming hole for dogs as well as frogs and tadpoles. Come explore this conservation land purchased by the town for Wellesley’s 1981 Centennial Celebration and now one of the most popular trails in Wellesley. This walk also includes Mass Bay land that is maintained by the Wellesley Trails Committee and the Wellesley DPW.

events.description: Rules of the Ride is a fun, hands-on way to learn bike handling and confidence for 5th to 8th graders. This year’s event is Sunday, June 7 from 10 a.m. to 11:30 a.m. at the Wellesley High School parking lot. Participants will learn how to ride safely and how to legally use critical skills training from MassBike instructors. The event includes a guided ride led by the Wellesley Police Department. Participants need a rideable bike, closed-toed shoes, and a helmet. Registration is required. (use Activity #231505).

You’ll have time to create a small collage piece! We’ll provide blank paper, scissors, and magazines for clipping. You’re also welcome to bring your own magazines. Other materials like newspapers, discarded books, or copies of photographs also make great sources for collage.

events.title: Call for Walkers: Register for the 38th Annual Jimmy Fund Walk for Dana-Farber presented by Hyundai
events.description: Registration is open for the 2026 Jimmy Fund Walk for Dana-Farber presented by Hyundai. Scheduled for Sunday, Oct. 4, funds raised from the Walk support all forms of adult and pediatric care and cancer research at Dana-Farber Cancer Institute. The 2026 event will aim to raise $10 million in support of Dana-Farber’s mission to defy cancer by leading the way in groundbreaking research and lifesaving patient care. The Details: The Jimmy Fund Walk is the only organized walk permitted to use the famed Boston Marathon® course, and participants have the flexibility to choose from four distance options: 5K walk: Starting at Dana-Farber’s Longwood Medical Campus 10K walk: Starting in Newton Half Marathon walk: Starting in Wellesley Marathon walk: Starting in Hopkinton Along the route, participants will be motivated by Hero posters, photos and quotes from Dana-Farber patients, who are considered the heart and inspiration for the event. Walkers will also enjoy refreshments and cheer squads throughout the course. The event will conclude at the Boston Common Finish Line, by the corner of Charles and Beacon Streets. Finish line activities will include a celebration with food, entertainment, and more. Walkers who prefer to participate virtually can complete their chosen distance from any location. To register for the Jimmy Fund Walk for Dana-Farber or to support a walker, visit JimmyFund.org/Walk or call (866) 531-9255. Registrants can enter the promo code NEWS for $5 off the registration fee. You can also sign up to volunteer on Walk Day.

events.title: Art & the Family Archive | The Persistence of Family with Diana Cheren Nygren
events.description: Join Diana Cheren Nygren for a talk about breathing new life into the family archive at a time when photographs are everywhere but the traditional family photo album is disappearing. What can we learn about ourselves from our history? It is a well known cliche that ancestors worked and acted in hopes of building a better future for their descendants. But what does this mean once the possibility of a better future all but disappears? Does that change our relationship to the past as well as the future?
